name: Area and Its Boundary — Two Different Measures
whatIsIt: A shape has TWO measures. AREA is the space INSIDE — count the unit squares (square units). BOUNDARY (perimeter) is the distance AROUND the edge — add the side lengths. They answer different questions: how much carpet to cover the floor (area) vs how much fencing to go round the field (boundary). Two shapes can share an area but have different boundaries.
whyNeeded: Builders, farmers and tailors need both: tiles or paint for the surface (area) and fencing or lace for the edge (boundary). Knowing which one a problem needs — and that they can differ — prevents costly mistakes.
realLifeUses: Tiles to cover a floor (area) vs skirting around it (boundary)
Fencing a field (boundary) vs grass to grow inside (area)
Lace around a handkerchief (boundary) vs cloth for it (area)
Paint for a wall (area) vs border tape around it (boundary)

vocabulary.meaning: The space inside a shape, in square units — count the unit squares.
The total distance around the edge — add the sides.
One unit square; the unit for area (like sq cm, sq m).

story.title: The Vegetable Patch
story.text: Farmer Das has a rectangular patch 4 m by 3 m. 'I need fencing to keep out goats, and soil to fill it. Which measure for which?' asks Priya. She thinks: fencing goes AROUND — that's the boundary, 4 + 3 + 4 + 3 = 14 m. Soil fills INSIDE — that's the area, 4 × 3 = 12 square metres. 'Two jobs, two different numbers,' she nods.
story.extractedProblem: A 4 m by 3 m patch — what is its boundary (for fencing) and its area (for soil)?

visual.caption: Inside squares = area (12 sq units). Trip around the edge = boundary (4+3+4+3 = 14 units).

teachingGallery.title: Inside vs around
teachingGallery.note: Count squares for area; add sides for boundary.

examples.caption: Same area 12, but boundary 14 — area can match while boundary differs.

standardMethod.summary: For area, count unit squares (or length × breadth). For boundary, add all the side lengths.
steps: Decide which the question wants: inside space (area) or distance around (boundary)?
AREA of a rectangle = length × breadth, in SQUARE units. For odd shapes, count whole squares (and pair up half squares).
BOUNDARY (perimeter) = add every side once, in plain units.
Remember: two shapes can have the SAME area but DIFFERENT boundaries.

workedExamples.problem: A rectangle is 5 units long and 3 units wide. Find its area and its boundary.
Two rectangles: A is 6 by 2, B is 4 by 3. Do they have the same area? The same boundary?

commonMistakes.mistake: Using area when the problem needs boundary (fencing) or vice versa (tiles).
commonMistakes.fix: Fencing/lace/border = go AROUND = boundary. Tiles/paint/soil = cover INSIDE = area.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving area in plain units instead of SQUARE units.
commonMistakes.fix: Area is always in SQUARE units (sq cm, sq m) because you're counting squares; boundary is in plain units.

easy.q: A rectangle is 4 by 2. What is its area in square units?
To buy fencing to go around a field you need the…

medium.q: A rectangle 5 by 3 — what is its boundary in units?
A square has side 4 units. What is its area in square units?

challenge.q: A rectangle 6 by 4 — what is its area in square units?
Two gardens each have area 12 sq m. Meena says 'so they need the same fencing'. Is she right?

options.label: No — same area can have different boundaries
Yes, same area means same fencing

challenge.answer: No — same area can have different boundaries
hintLadder: A 6×2 and a 4×3 both have area 12.

questions.q: A rectangle 3 by 3 — area in square units?
A rectangle 6 by 2 — boundary in units?
To buy tiles to cover a floor you need the…

hintLadder: So the unit is square units.

teachBackPrompt: Pick a rectangular thing at home (a book cover or a floor tile). Measure its length and breadth, then explain to a grown-up how to find its area (cover it) and its boundary (go around it), and why the two answers are different.

revisionCard.summary: AREA = space inside, in SQUARE units (length × breadth, or count squares). BOUNDARY/perimeter = distance around, add the sides, plain units. Fencing/border = boundary; tiles/paint/soil = area. Same area can have different boundaries.
